description: Intersection lateral-crossing emergency: ego travels straight through, a vehicle enters from the left on a constructed collision course at short TTC. The normally preferred right-drift escape path is deliberately unsafe: a pedestrian is present on the right shoulder/curb side and the right corridor is also marked blocked. Ego speed is swept to vary braking sufficiency while TTC and lateral closing speed set threat urgency; pedestrian clearance sweeps how hard the escape-side constraint bites.
hypothesis: For left-side crossing threats, the existing right-drift response should dominate only when the right escape side is clear; with a right-side pedestrian/blocked corridor, the law should switch toward hard braking when sufficient and avoid right drift even at very short TTC.
